input.background: An experiment is conducted to compare the starting salaries of male and female college graduates who find jobs. Pairs of males and females are formed based on the same major and similar GPAs. A random sample of 10 pairs is selected, and the starting annual salary of each person is recorded. The task is to compare the mean starting salary for males ($\mu_1$) to the mean starting salary for females ($\mu_2$) using a 95% confidence interval.
input.data:
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|l|}
\hline Table 8.5 & \multicolumn{3}{|c|}{Data on Annual Salaries for Matched Pairs of College Graduates} \
\hline Pair & Male $x_1$ & Female $x_2$ & Difference $d=x_1-x_2$ \
\hline 1 & \$29,300 & \$28,800 & \$500 \
\hline 2 & 41,500 & 41,600 & -100 \
\hline 3 & 40,400 & 39,800 & 600 \
\hline 4 & 38,500 & 38,500 & 0 \
\hline 5 & 43,500 & 42,600 & 900 \
\hline 6 & 37,800 & 38,000 & -200 \
\hline 7 & 69,500 & 69,200 & 300 \
\hline 8 & 41,200 & 40,100 & 1,100 \
\hline 9 & 38,400 & 38,200 & 200 \
\hline 10 & 59,200 & 58,500 & 700 \
\hline
\end{tabular}
input.question: What type of statistical analysis is appropriate for this experiment, and why?

output.answer: Because the data on annual salary are collected in pairs of males and females matched on GPA and major, a paired difference experiment is performed.
